title: AGENTS.md outperforms skills in our agent evals - Vercel
author: Jude GaoSoftware Engineer, Next.js
summary:
The development of AI coding agents has introduced a new challenge: keeping their training data up-to-date with the latest framework-specific knowledge. Next.js 16, for example, introduces new APIs that are not yet included in the training data of current models. To address this issue, two approaches were tested: using "skills" to package domain knowledge that agents can invoke on demand, and embedding a compressed documentation index directly in a markdown file called AGENTS.md. The skills approach seemed promising, as it allows agents to access relevant documentation when working on specific tasks. However, the results were surprising, with the AGENTS.md approach achieving a 100% pass rate, outperforming the skills-based approach even with explicit instructions.

The skills approach was found to have several limitations. Without explicit instructions, the agent often failed to invoke the skill, resulting in no improvement over having no documentation at all. Even with instructions, the wording was found to be fragile, with subtle changes producing dramatically different outcomes. In contrast, the AGENTS.md approach provided consistent and reliable results, eliminating the need for the agent to decide when to invoke a skill. The compressed documentation index, which was reduced to 8KB, allowed the agent to access version-accurate information without having to load the full documentation. This approach was found to work well for general framework knowledge, while skills may still be useful for vertical, action-specific workflows that users explicitly trigger.

The results of this experiment have important implications for framework authors and developers. They suggest that providing an AGENTS.md snippet that users can add to their projects can significantly improve the performance of coding agents. The AGENTS.md approach also highlights the importance of designing documentation for retrieval, rather than assuming that agents will be able to find the relevant information on their own. By shifting the focus from pre-training-led reasoning to retrieval-led reasoning, developers can create more effective and efficient coding agents. The study's findings can be applied to other frameworks and coding agents, and the code snippet is available for use in Next.js projects.

title: AI is Killing B2B SaaS
author: namanyayg
summary:
The Software as a Service (SaaS) business model has been incredibly profitable, with the ability to build a product once and sell it repeatedly without incurring significant marginal costs. However, the rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and "vibe coding" is posing an existential threat to B2B SaaS companies. Vibe coding allows customers to build their own customized tools and workflows using AI-powered platforms, which can be more flexible and cost-effective than traditional SaaS solutions. This has led to a decline in the value of SaaS companies, with many analysts questioning the need to own software stocks.

The problem for B2B SaaS companies is that customers are no longer willing to settle for off-the-shelf solutions that don't meet their specific needs. With vibe coding, customers can create their own custom tools and workflows, which can be more efficient and effective than traditional SaaS solutions. However, these custom-built solutions often lack the robustness, security, and scalability of established SaaS platforms. To survive, B2B SaaS companies need to adapt to this new reality and find ways to provide more flexible and customizable solutions to their customers.

There are several strategies that B2B SaaS companies can use to survive in this new environment. One approach is to become a "System of Record," where the company's platform is deeply integrated into the customer's workflow and is essential to their daily operations. Another approach is to focus on security, authentication, and robustness, which are areas where vibe-coded apps often fall short. Established SaaS platforms have spent years developing robust security protocols and compliance certifications, which can provide a competitive advantage in the market. Finally, B2B SaaS companies need to become more adaptable and customizable, allowing customers to build on top of their platforms rather than trying to force customers to use a one-size-fits-all solution.

The shift towards more flexible and customizable solutions is driven by the fact that customers have tasted the possibilities of AI-powered workflow automation and are no longer willing to settle for less. The survivors in the B2B SaaS market will be the companies that can evolve and become platforms that allow customers to build on top of them, rather than trying to sell them a pre-built solution. This requires a fundamental shift in the way that SaaS companies interact with their customers, from a focus on selling a product to a focus on providing a platform for customers to build and create. By providing a whitelabelled AI platform that allows customers to vibe code customized workflows on top of their existing system of record, B2B SaaS companies can support retention, engagement, and expansion, and thrive in a market that is increasingly driven by customer demand for flexibility and customization.

title: AI Made Writing Code Easier. It Made Engineering Harder.
author: ivan.turkovic
summary:
The software engineering landscape has undergone a significant transformation with the advent of AI-powered tools. While these tools have made writing code easier and faster, they have also introduced a new set of challenges that are often overlooked. The reality is that the day-to-day life of software engineers has become more complex, demanding, and exhausting. The barrier to producing code may have decreased, but the expectations of what engineers can accomplish have increased exponentially. This shift has occurred without a formal acknowledgement or adjustment of job roles, leaving many engineers feeling overwhelmed and undervalued.

The introduction of AI tools has created a phenomenon where the baseline of expected output has moved dramatically. Engineers are now expected to produce more code, work at a faster pace, and take on broader tasks. A study by Harvard Business Review found that 83% of workers reported an increase in workload, and 62% of associates and 61% of entry-level workers experienced burnout. This is in stark contrast to the perception of leadership, who may view AI as a means to increase productivity without fully understanding the toll it takes on engineers. The gap between the expectations of leadership and the reality of engineers' experiences is eroding trust, morale, and eventually talent.

The shift towards AI-assisted engineering has also led to an identity crisis for many engineers. They are being asked to move away from the creative act of writing code, which is what drew them to the profession in the first place. Instead, they are expected to focus on higher-level tasks, such as directing systems that produce code. This feels like a loss of professional identity, as engineers are being asked to redefine what they do and who they are without any transition period or acknowledgement of the significance of this change. The expansion of the engineer's role to include more product thinking, architectural decision-making, and code review has added to the complexity and scope of the job, leading to burnout and decreased productivity.

The supervision paradox is another challenge that arises from AI-generated code. Reviewing code produced by AI is often harder than writing it yourself, as the context and reasoning behind the code are not immediately apparent. This leads to increased time spent debugging and reviewing code, which is a structural property of the workflow. The faster AI generates code, the more human attention is required to ensure it works in the context of a real system. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle where AI makes tasks faster, but the increased workload and complexity lead to more AI reliance, which in turn leads to more code that needs review and maintenance.

The acceleration trap is a self-reinforcing cycle where AI removes the natural governor on production, allowing engineers to produce more code than ever before. However, this increased production comes at the cost of cognitive endurance, as engineers are pushed to work at an unsustainable pace. The only limit is now cognitive endurance, which most people do not know until they have already exceeded it. This has led many engineers to find themselves shipping more code than ever before, but at the cost of their well-being and job satisfaction. The industry must acknowledge and address these challenges to prevent the erosion of trust, morale, and talent that is currently underway.

title: Amplifying — AI Benchmark Research
author: Alex Vikati
summary:
The study, conducted by Edwin Ong and Alex Vikati, aimed to understand the decision-making process of Claude Code, a code generation tool, by analyzing its responses to open-ended questions across various project types and categories. The researchers ran the tool on 2,430 real repositories, using three different models - Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.5, and Opus 4.6 - and observed the tools and methods it recommended. One of the key findings was that Claude Code tends to "build" rather than "buy," meaning it often prefers custom or DIY solutions over existing tools. This was evident in 12 out of 20 categories, where the tool opted for custom implementations, such as using environment variables and percentage-based rollout for feature flags, or writing JWT and bcrypt authentication from scratch in Python.

When Claude Code did recommend tools, it showed a strong preference for certain ones, such as GitHub Actions, Stripe, and shadcn/ui. The study also revealed that the tool's recommendations can shape the default technology stack for many applications, particularly in the JavaScript ecosystem. For instance, Zustand and Sentry were found to be strong defaults for state management and observability, respectively. However, the tool also showed a tendency to ignore certain popular tools, such as those for API layer management and testing. The researchers noted that newer models tend to pick newer tools, indicating a recency gradient in the recommendations. Furthermore, the study found that deployment choices are often determined by the technology stack, with Vercel and Railway being the preferred choices for JavaScript and Python projects, respectively.

The study's findings have significant implications for devtool companies, as they can provide insights into how AI agents recommend their tools compared to competitors. The researchers offer private dashboards and benchmarking services to help companies understand where they stand in the market and identify areas for improvement. Overall, the study provides a detailed analysis of Claude Code's decision-making process and its potential impact on the technology industry.

title: Amy’s Drive Thru to close Rohnert Park location
author: KTVU Staff
summary:
Amy's Drive Thru, a pioneering vegetarian fast-food chain, has announced the closure of its Rohnert Park location, which was the company's first drive-thru restaurant. The location, which has been in operation for 10 years, is set to close its doors for good on March 8. This closure marks the end of an era for the company, which had previously closed its other three drive-thru locations over the past year, including the most recent one in Corte Madera. In a statement, the company reflected on its mission to serve delicious, organic food to everyone, and expressed pride in the countless meals, smiles, and memories shared with its customers.

The company also took the opportunity to thank its employees, whom it referred to as "the heart of Amy's Drive Thru." While the closure of the Rohnert Park location may seem like the end of the road for Amy's Drive Thru, the company is actually shifting its focus towards a new direction. Amy's will continue to operate a restaurant inside San Francisco International Airport, and plans to expand its presence in grocery stores across the US by manufacturing and selling its products. This move signals a significant change in the company's strategy, as it looks to make its products more widely available to customers beyond its physical restaurant locations. As the company embarks on this new chapter, it will undoubtedly be remembered for its innovative approach to vegetarian fast food and its commitment to serving high-quality, organic meals to its customers.

Animated Avif for the Modern Web | Arthur Pizza
author: Arthur Pizza
summary:
The concept of animating images has been around for a while, with GIFs being a popular choice. However, GIFs have become somewhat outdated and are not optimized for the modern web. Fortunately, a better alternative has emerged in the form of AVIF, which has an animation mode. This format offers a more efficient solution for creating animated images. To create an animated AVIF file, users can utilize FFMPEG, a powerful tool that can handle video processing. The process involves clipping a piece of video or using an existing animated GIF as a starting point.

To create an animated AVIF file, users can employ a multi-step process using FFMPEG. First, they need to convert their input video into a compatible format, such as Y4M. This can be achieved by using the following command: `ffmpeg -i INPUT_VIDEO.webm -pix_fmt yuv420p -an -y output.y4m`. Users can also limit the size and framerate of the output by adding additional parameters, such as `fps=15` and `scale=720:-1`. Once the video is in the correct format, it can be encoded into an AVIF file using another FFMPEG command: `ffmpeg -y -i output.y4m -c:v libsvtav1 -crf 30 -b:v 0 -an -y output.avif`. While it may be possible to combine these steps into a single command, some users may encounter issues, making it necessary to stage the process to a Y4M file first.

Overall, creating animated AVIF files offers a promising alternative to traditional GIFs, providing a more modern and efficient way to display animated images on the web. With the help of FFMPEG and a few simple commands, users can create their own animated AVIF files and take advantage of this exciting new format. As technology continues to evolve, it is likely that the process will become even more streamlined, making it easier for users to create and share animated content.

title: Ars Technica Fires Reporter After AI Controversy Involving Fabricated Quotes
author: Maggie Harrison Dupré
summary:
The recent controversy surrounding Ars Technica, a Condé Nast-owned publication, has shed light on the challenges of integrating artificial intelligence (AI) in journalism. The incident began with the retraction of an article that included AI-fabricated quotes attributed to a real person, Scott Shambaugh. The article, which was initially published on February 13, was a write-up of a viral incident involving an AI agent and a human engineer. However, Shambaugh pointed out that he had never said the quotes attributed to him, leading to a retraction and an apology from Ars Technica's editor-in-chief, Ken Fisher.

The retraction was followed by a statement from senior AI reporter Benj Edwards, who took full responsibility for the inclusion of the fabricated quotes. Edwards explained that he had been using an experimental AI tool to help him extract relevant source material while working from home with a fever. However, the tool failed to work, and he inadvertently used ChatGPT to paraphrase Shambaugh's words instead of using his actual quotes. Edwards emphasized that the article was human-written and that the incident was isolated, but the damage had already been done. The controversy led to a wave of pushback and speculation from Ars readers, who expressed frustration and disappointment in a lengthy comment thread.

The incident ultimately led to Edwards' termination from Ars Technica, as confirmed by Futurism. The publication's creative director, Aurich Lawson, stated that internal steps had been taken, but the company did not comment on personnel decisions. The retraction and Edwards' termination highlight the risks of relying on AI in journalism, even for those who are familiar with its shortcomings. As Edwards noted, the irony of an AI reporter being tripped up by AI hallucination is not lost on him, and the incident serves as a painful reminder of the importance of accuracy and human judgment in journalism. The controversy also underscores the need for clear guidelines on the use of AI in journalism, as the media industry navigates a complex and ever-shifting landscape of AI-generated content, copyright battles, and changing audience habits.

title: Artificial Intelligence Broke Interviews
Avoid Mini-frameworks
summary:
The author of this article, who has worked in Google Ads infrastructure for four years, warns against the creation of "mini-frameworks" - small, custom frameworks built on top of a company's shared tech stack. These frameworks often emerge as a solution to specific pain points or to simplify the use of the shared stack, but they can cause more problems than they solve. The author defines a mini-framework as a framework created by a small team to address their specific needs, which introduces new concepts and claims to magically solve many problems. However, these frameworks often lack feature completeness and compatibility, violating the "Easier To Change" principle, and can lead to tech stack fragmentation, maintenance issues, and frustration for developers who have to use them.

The author shares their personal experience of working with a mini-framework, which was created to lower the barrier for adoption of a Google-internal framework. Despite initial enthusiasm, the framework proved to be difficult to use, introduced unnecessary complexity, and hindered the development process. The author argues that the fundamental problem lies in creating the mini-framework in the first place, rather than addressing the underlying issues with the shared tech stack. They also observe that mini-frameworks often reflect the creator's mental model, but not everyone's, leading to a lack of understanding and adoption.

The author identifies several reasons why mini-frameworks are bad, including their lack of feature completeness, violation of the "Easier To Change" principle, introduction of unnecessary complexity, and tendency to cause tech stack fragmentation. They also note that mini-frameworks often lack maintenance, as they are typically owned by a small team or individual, and can become obsolete when the creators leave the company. To avoid these issues, the author suggests that developers should be cautious when introducing new concepts and avoid creating mini-frameworks whenever possible. Instead, they recommend creating libraries that build upon the existing framework without introducing new concepts. If a framework is necessary, they advise linking concepts to concrete business requirements, starting from scratch, and taking the decision seriously.

The author concludes by emphasizing the importance of being mindful of the distinction between libraries and frameworks and treating the decision to create a framework as a serious one. By doing so, developers can avoid creating unnecessary mini-frameworks and instead focus on building solutions that are maintainable, scalable, and easy to use. Ultimately, the author's goal is to raise awareness about the potential pitfalls of mini-frameworks and encourage developers to think carefully before creating new frameworks that may not be necessary or may even cause harm.

title: Build and publish packages - Flox Docs
BuildKit: Docker's Hidden Gem That Can Build Almost Anything
summary:
BuildKit is a powerful, general-purpose build framework that underlies many everyday interactions, particularly when using Docker. While it's commonly associated with building Dockerfiles, its capabilities extend far beyond that. At its core, BuildKit is designed to produce a wide range of outputs, including OCI images, tarballs, local directories, and even APK packages. This versatility is achieved through its modular architecture, which consists of three key concepts: LLB (Low-Level Build definition), frontends, and the solver and cache. LLB is an intermediate representation that describes a directed acyclic graph of filesystem operations, allowing for content-addressable and cacheable builds.

The frontend is a pluggable layer that can be customized to read various build definitions, such as Dockerfiles, YAML, JSON, or even custom domain-specific languages (DSLs). This means that users can write their own frontends to translate their specifications into LLB, which can then be executed by BuildKit. The solver takes the LLB graph and executes it, leveraging content-addressing to skip redundant operations and cache results. This results in faster builds and improved reproducibility. The cache can be local, inline, or remote, making it easy to share builds across continuous integration (CI) runners.

One of the most exciting aspects of BuildKit is its ability to produce outputs beyond container images. The `--output` flag allows users to export the result as a local directory, tarball, or OCI image tarball, among other formats. This feature has significant implications for non-image use cases, such as building compiled binaries, packages, or documentation. To demonstrate this, a custom frontend was built to produce Alpine APK packages from a YAML specification, showcasing the flexibility and potential of BuildKit.

The benefits of using BuildKit are substantial. By leveraging its content-addressable, parallelized, and cached build engine, developers can focus on writing their build specifications without worrying about the underlying execution mechanics. This approach has already been proven at scale by projects like Dagger, Earthly, and Depot, which have built their execution engines on top of BuildKit's LLB. As a result, BuildKit has become an attractive option for building tools that require compiling code, producing artifacts, or orchestrating multi-step builds. By harnessing the power of BuildKit, developers can streamline their build processes, improve reproducibility, and reduce the complexity associated with building and deploying software.

Coding After Coders: The End of Computer Programming as We Know It
author: Clive Thompson
summary:
In the era of artificial intelligence (A.I.), the role of software developers is undergoing a significant transformation. Many programmers in Silicon Valley are no longer writing code from scratch, but instead, are working with A.I. agents that can generate code at an unprecedented speed and accuracy. Manu Ebert, a machine-learning engineer and founder of the start-up Hyperspell, is one such example. He uses Claude Code, an A.I. tool from Anthropic, to develop software, and his experience is not unusual. Ebert and his colleague, Conor Brennan-Burke, now spend their days describing what they want the A.I. to do, and the agents translate that idea into code.

This shift in coding has significant implications for the profession. For decades, coding was considered a highly skilled and lucrative job, with programmers spending hours, even days, writing and debugging code. However, with A.I. taking over the drudgery of coding, the role of developers is becoming more conversational, with humans providing high-level instructions and the A.I. agents handling the implementation details. This has led to a significant increase in productivity, with some developers reporting being 10 to 20 times more productive than before. The A.I. agents can also experiment and try out different approaches, allowing developers to focus on the overall shape and design of the software.

While some might view this as a threat to the profession, many developers are embracing the change. They see the A.I. agents as tools that can free them from the mundane and time-consuming aspects of coding, allowing them to focus on the creative and soulful parts of the job. Anil Dash, a longtime programmer and tech executive, notes that A.I. is taking away the drudgery of coding, leaving the human, soulful parts to the developers. This is in contrast to other creative disciplines, where A.I. is often seen as a threat, as it can automate tasks that were previously considered the exclusive domain of humans.

The use of A.I. in coding has also raised questions about the nature of computation and coding. Boris Cherny, the head of Claude Code, asks, "What is computation — what is coding?" The answer, according to many developers, is that coding is no longer just about writing code, but about designing and architecting software. Developers are now more like architects, focusing on the overall design and functionality of the software, rather than the implementation details. This shift has significant implications for the future of the profession, and it will be interesting to see how it evolves in the coming years.

title: County pays $600,000 to pentesters it arrested for assessing courthouse security
author: Dan Goodin
summary:
In a significant settlement, two security professionals, Gary DeMercurio and Justin Wynn, will receive $600,000 to resolve a lawsuit they filed against authorities in Iowa. The lawsuit stemmed from their arrest in 2019, despite having written authorization to conduct a security assessment, also known as a "red-team" exercise, at a county courthouse. As penetration testers employed by Coalfire Labs, DeMercurio and Wynn had been hired by the Iowa Judicial Branch to test the resilience of the courthouse's defenses using real-world attack techniques. The rules of engagement for the exercise explicitly permitted physical attacks, including lockpicking, as long as no significant damage was caused.

The incident began when the two men found an unlocked side door to the courthouse, which they then closed and locked. They used a makeshift tool to trip the locking mechanism and gain entry, triggering an alarm that alerted authorities. Despite their legitimate work and the prior authorization, DeMercurio and Wynn were arrested and charged with felony third-degree burglary, spending 20 hours in jail before being released on $100,000 bail. The charges were later reduced to misdemeanor trespassing, but the damage to their reputation had already been done. The sheriff of Dallas County continued to publicly allege that the men had acted illegally, sending a chilling message to security professionals nationwide that helping governments identify vulnerabilities could lead to arrest, prosecution, and public disgrace.

The settlement is a significant victory for DeMercurio and Wynn, who argued that their arrest and subsequent charges were wrongful and defamatory. In a statement, Wynn emphasized that the incident did not make anyone safer, but rather undermined public safety by discouraging security professionals from conducting essential vulnerability testing. The case highlights the importance of clear communication and understanding between security professionals, government agencies, and law enforcement to ensure that authorized security assessments are not mistakenly perceived as malicious activities. The settlement serves as a reminder of the need for greater awareness and cooperation between these parties to promote public safety and protect the reputation of security professionals.

Don't become an Engineering Manager
author: Anton Zaides
summary:
A friend recently confided in me about being offered a promotion to an Engineering Manager role, but was hesitant to accept it. This conversation led to a reevaluation of my own stance on accepting management roles, which I previously believed to be a valuable experience for engineers. However, after discussing the tradeoffs, I agreed with my friend that he should decline the offer. Our conversation centered around the rapidly changing landscape of the industry, where the traditional career ladder is no longer a sure thing. With companies flattening their organizational structures, there are fewer opportunities for growth and advancement in management roles.

The traditional Engineering Manager ladder, which once looked like a clear progression from EM to Senior EM to Director to VP, is no longer a reliable path. Companies like Amazon have increased their IC-to-manager ratio, resulting in fewer Director and VP roles available. This means that even experienced leaders may find themselves stuck or competing with others for limited spots. Furthermore, the compensation for management roles may not be as lucrative as previously thought. My friend, for instance, discovered that he could earn more as a Senior or Staff Engineer at another startup than he would as an EM at his current company. In fact, being a Staff engineer is often better paid across the industry due to high demand.

As I reflected on our conversation, I realized that my own enthusiasm for management roles was not entirely rational. While I believe that experienced Engineering Managers can still thrive in this new landscape, I also acknowledge that being an IC can be a smarter choice in today's job market. However, I must confess that my own enjoyment of my job plays a significant role in my decision-making. I would advise senior engineers to exercise caution when considering a jump to management, at least for the next couple of years, to see how the industry continues to evolve. Ultimately, the decision should be guided by one's passion and gut feeling, rather than just rational considerations.

title: Don't paste raw LLM output at people
summary:
The proliferation of Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly altered the way we approach writing and communication. Sharing raw AI output can be detrimental to both the sender and the recipient, as it disrupts the traditionally balanced relationship between the effort required to produce and consume text. Prior to LLMs, authors invested time and effort in crafting their words, which was reciprocated by the audience's effort in reading and understanding the text. However, with LLMs, the production of text has become virtually effortless, while the effort required to read and comprehend it remains unchanged. This asymmetry is further exacerbated by the increasing verbosity of LLMs, which can lead to a phenomenon known as "sloppypasta," where the raw AI output overwhelms the conversation and blocks the viewport.

The consequences of relying on LLMs extend beyond the disruption of effort balance. Writing is an integral part of the thinking process, as it forces authors to work through their thoughts and build their comprehension and retention. By delegating tasks to LLMs, individuals create cognitive debt, ultimately reducing their understanding and recall of the subject matter. Moreover, LLMs lack personal expertise and perspective, which were previously essential components of written communication. As a result, trust can no longer be assumed, and readers are burdened with a "verification tax," where they must scrutinize the information to determine its accuracy. The authoritative tone and confidence of LLMs can further obscure the sender's actual level of expertise, eroding trust and making it challenging for readers to distinguish between credible and unreliable information.

The erosion of trust is a significant concern, as it undermines the foundation of effective communication. In the past, trust was the default, and readers could gauge an author's understanding of a subject based on the coherence of their writing. However, with LLMs, the chain of trust is obfuscated, making it difficult to determine who is responsible for errors or inaccuracies. This uncertainty creates a compounding negative feedback loop, where the sender forfeits learning and credibility, while the recipient burns effort and loses trust. As highlighted by experts such as Alex Martsinovich, Blake Stockton, and Simon Willison, sharing raw AI output can be seen as rude, dismissive, or unprofessional, and it is essential to prioritize the reader's experience and invest time in crafting thoughtful and accurate communication. Ultimately, the responsible use of LLMs requires a nuanced understanding of their limitations and a commitment to maintaining the integrity of human communication.

title: How dark web agent spotted bedroom wall clue to rescue girl from abuse
author: Sam Piranty
summary:
Greg Squire, a specialist online investigator, and his team at the US Department of Homeland Security Investigations, have dedicated their work to identifying and rescuing children who have been victims of sexual abuse. One particular case that stands out is that of a 12-year-old girl named Lucy, whose disturbing images were being shared on the dark web. Despite the abuser's efforts to conceal identifying features, Squire and his team were determined to track down Lucy's location. They began by analyzing the images, looking for any clues that could lead them to her. They noticed the type of light sockets and electrical outlets, which indicated that Lucy was in North America, but that was about all they could deduce.

The team contacted Facebook, which was dominant in the social media landscape at the time, asking for help in scouring uploaded family photos to see if Lucy was in any of them. However, Facebook claimed that it did not have the tools to assist, despite having facial recognition technology. Undeterred, Squire and his team continued to analyze the images, looking for any element that might help. They discovered that a sofa seen in some of the images was only sold regionally, which narrowed down the search to about 40,000 people. However, this was still a daunting task, as it covered 29 states and tens of thousands of addresses.

The breakthrough came when the team noticed an exposed brick wall in Lucy's bedroom, which seemed like a mundane detail but ultimately proved to be crucial. Squire started searching online for brick experts and found the Brick Industry Association, which put him in touch with John Harp, a brick sales expert. Harp identified the brick as a "Flaming Alamo," which was only made by a specific company in the US southwest from the late 1960s to the mid-1980s. This information helped the team narrow down the search to a 100-mile radius of the brick factory, where they found a list of potential customers who had bought the sofa.

By trawling through social media, the team found a photo of Lucy with an adult who appeared to be a relative, which led them to the woman's address. They then used this information to find other addresses connected to the person and the people she had lived with, eventually narrowing down the possible location of Lucy's residence. To confirm the address, the team sent photos of the houses to Harp, who assessed whether the properties were likely to have been built during the period when Flaming Alamos were on sale. Finally, they found an address that matched the criteria, and local Homeland Security agents arrested the offender, a convicted sex offender who had been raping Lucy for six years. The perpetrator was subsequently sentenced to over 70 years in jail.

Squire's work on the case was instrumental in rescuing Lucy, but it also took a toll on his mental health. He has struggled with the pressure of his job, which involves exposure to horrific images and conversations with perpetrators. A few years ago, he began to experience suicidal thoughts, but with the support of his colleague Pete Manning, he sought help and began to recover. Last summer, Squire met Lucy, now in her 20s, for the first time, and she expressed her gratitude for the support she has received, which has enabled her to discuss her experiences and find stability. Squire's story highlights the importance of his work and the challenges that come with it, but also the impact it can have on the lives of victims like Lucy.

The blogger's account of his visit, which initially seems straightforward, becomes a captivating journey through the evolution of the English language.

As the blogger explores Wulfleet, he encounters strange occurrences, including a mysterious man who seems to be following him. His descriptions of the town and its people become more vivid and detailed, but also more formal and antiquated. The text transforms into a kind of historical pastiche, weaving together elements of different literary styles and periods. The blogger's tone shifts from casual and conversational to more formal and introspective, reflecting the changing language and literary conventions of each era. Despite the challenges of reading the increasingly archaic language, the story remains engaging, drawing the reader into the blogger's experiences and the strange, almost mythical world of Wulfleet.

The experiment is a remarkable demonstration of the English language's capacity for evolution and adaptation. By compressing a thousand years of linguistic development into a single blog post, the author creates a unique and captivating narrative that showcases the language's versatility and expressiveness. As the story unfolds, the reader is invited to reflect on the ways in which language shapes our perceptions and understanding of the world, and how it can be used to create rich, immersive, and imaginative experiences. Ultimately, the experiment becomes a celebration of the English language's power and beauty, and an invitation to explore its many facets and complexities.

summary:
The author of this text has been teaching their 9-pound cavapoo, Momo, to create video games using a unique system. The process involves Momo typing on a Bluetooth keyboard connected to a Raspberry Pi, which forwards the input to a language model called Claude Code. The key to making this work is to create a prompt that tells Claude Code to interpret Momo's random keystrokes as meaningful instructions. The author has developed a system that filters out special keys, rewards Momo with treats when she types enough, and provides automated feedback to Claude Code to help it refine its game development skills.

The author's motivation for this project began when they were working on a game prototype in Godot and discovered that Momo had been typing on their keyboard, generating strange input. Initially, they thought it was a prank, but later, after losing their job as a research engineer, they decided to explore the possibility of using Momo's input to create something meaningful. They started by creating a prompt that would convince Claude Code to take Momo's input seriously, telling it that a genius game designer was communicating in a cryptic language. After many iterations, they developed a system that could interpret Momo's keystrokes and turn them into playable games.

The system involves several components, including a Raspberry Pi that receives Momo's keystrokes and forwards them to Claude Code, a tool that filters out special keys and rewards Momo with treats when she types enough. The author also developed tools to provide automated feedback to Claude Code, such as a screenshot tool and a way to send sequences of input to running game instances. These tools have helped to reduce the number of games with errors and have enabled Claude Code to become its own QA tester. The author has also open-sourced some of the tools they developed, including a scene linter, shader linter, and input action mapper.

To train Momo, the author started by scattering high-value treats on the keyboard to build an association between the keyboard and rewards. Momo was initially frustrated but eventually learned to swipe her paw across the keyboard to generate input. The author then automated the rewards using a smart pet feeder controlled by a script that sends Zigbee commands. With the system in place, Momo can now create games that are playable and fun, and the author is excited to see what she will come up with next. Overall, the project has been a successful experiment in using machine learning and automation to enable a dog to create something meaningful and entertaining.

author: Abhishek Ray
summary:
The increasing use of autonomous systems, such as AI-powered code generation tools like Gastown and Claude, has raised concerns about the reliability and accuracy of the code they produce. As these systems can generate code rapidly without human oversight, the need for reliable verification processes has become more pressing. The traditional method of code review, where a second set of eyes reviews the code, is no longer sufficient, especially when the same AI is used for both generating and reviewing the code. This is because the AI is essentially checking its own work, which can lead to undetected errors and misunderstandings.

The problem of trusting AI-generated code is not unique to individual developers, as teams and organizations are also struggling with this issue. The use of Claude for everyday pull requests has increased the number of merges, but it has also led to a significant amount of time spent on code reviews. As systems become more autonomous, the problem compounds, and the need for a reliable verification process becomes even more critical. One approach that has been proposed is to use Test-Driven Development (TDD) principles, where the test is written before the code, to ensure that the code meets the required specifications. However, this approach requires a significant amount of time and effort to think through what the code should do before writing it.

A more practical solution is to write acceptance criteria, which describe what the feature should do in plain English, before the agent builds it. This approach allows the machine to figure out how to check the code, making it easier to verify its accuracy. For example, when building a login feature, the acceptance criteria might include successful login, wrong password error, empty field validation, and rate limiting. Each criterion is specific enough that it either passes or fails, and the agent can build the feature and run verification tests against each criterion. This approach has been implemented using a Claude Skill, called Verify, which runs using Claude's headless mode and Playwright MCP. The workflow involves writing acceptance criteria before prompting the agent, building the feature, running verification, and reviewing only the failures.

The use of acceptance criteria and automated verification has several benefits, including the ability to catch integration failures, rendering bugs, and behavior that works in theory but breaks in a real browser. However, it is essential to acknowledge that this approach does not catch spec misunderstandings, where the specification itself is incorrect. Nevertheless, it provides a more reliable way to verify AI-generated code than traditional code review. By using acceptance criteria and automated verification, developers can ensure that their code meets the required specifications and reduce the risk of errors and misunderstandings.

Is legal the same as legitimate: AI reimplementation and the erosion of copyleft
summary:
The recent release of chardet version 7.0, a Python library for detecting text encodings, has sparked a debate in the open-source community. The library's maintainer, Dan Blanchard, used AI assistance to reimplement the library from scratch, resulting in a new version that is 48 times faster and supports multiple cores. However, the license was changed from LGPL to MIT, which has raised concerns among some developers, including the library's original author, Mark Pilgrim. Pilgrim argues that the reimplementation, although legally permissible, cannot be considered a clean-room effort due to the exposure to the original codebase.

The debate has drawn responses from prominent figures in the open-source world, including Armin Ronacher, the creator of Flask, and Salvatore Sanfilippo, the creator of Redis. Both Ronacher and Sanfilippo argue that the reimplementation is legitimate, with Sanfilippo grounding his argument in copyright law and the history of the GNU project. However, the author of the essay disagrees, arguing that the question of whether the reimplementation is legitimate is not answered by simply establishing its legality. The author contends that the analogy with the GNU project is flawed, as the vector of the reimplementation in the chardet case runs in the opposite direction, removing the copyleft protection that guaranteed users the right to study, modify, and redistribute derivative works under the same terms.

Ronacher's argument that the GPL runs against the spirit of sharing is also disputed by the author. The author argues that the GPL does not restrict sharing, but rather imposes a condition on sharing: if you share, you must share in kind. The requirement that improvements be returned to the commons is not a mechanism that suppresses sharing, but rather one that makes sharing recursive and self-reinforcing. The author also points out that Ronacher's stance on the issue is self-serving, as he has a personal stake in the outcome and is using a concept of sharing with a specific directionality built in, where sharing flows toward whoever has more capital and engineers to take advantage of it.

The author concludes that the distinction between legality and social legitimacy is crucial in this debate. While the reimplementation may be legally permissible, it does not necessarily mean that it is socially legitimate. The author argues that the erosion of copyleft protection, made possible by AI reimplementation, is a threat to the freedom of users and the norms of the open-source community. The principle that those who take from the commons owe something back to the commons remains unchanged, regardless of whether a reimplementation takes five years or five days. Ultimately, the author suggests that the open-source community needs to reexamine its norms and values in the face of emerging technologies like AI, and to consider the long-term implications of allowing copyleft protection to be eroded.

Kasava - The Agentic Platform for Product Engineers
author: Kasava
summary:
The conventional approach to artificial intelligence (AI) has been misguided, with companies often treating it as an autonomous agent that can "just figure it out." However, this approach has led to disappointment, as AI systems are not capable of understanding context and making judgment calls without human intervention. A more effective approach is to view AI as an extension of the human workforce, amplifying human capabilities rather than replacing them. This perspective is exemplified by the concept of an exoskeleton, a device that provides mechanical assistance to humans, enhancing their strength and endurance.

The exoskeleton model is not just a metaphor, but a real-world example of how technology can augment human capabilities. In manufacturing, logistics, military, and healthcare, exoskeletons have been deployed to reduce injuries, improve worker efficiency, and enhance productivity. For instance, Ford has deployed EksoVest exoskeletons in 15 plants, resulting in an 83% decrease in injuries. Similarly, German Bionic's Cray X exoskeleton provides up to 66 pounds of lift support, reducing worker fatigue and improving overall well-being. These examples demonstrate how technology can be designed to amplify human capabilities, rather than replace them.

In the context of AI, the exoskeleton model suggests that AI systems should be designed to assist humans, rather than replace them. This approach is exemplified by Kasava, a platform that provides deep research and analysis, but leaves decision-making to humans. Kasava's AI capabilities, such as commit analysis and transcript analysis, provide insights and patterns that humans may not be able to detect manually, but ultimately, it is the human who interprets the results and makes informed decisions. This symbiosis between human judgment and AI capabilities is what makes the exoskeleton model effective.

To build AI that actually works for teams, it is essential to decompose jobs into discrete tasks, identify areas where AI can amplify human capabilities, and keep humans in the decision loop. The micro-agent architecture, which involves building focused and reliable AI components, is a framework for achieving this. By making the seams visible and ensuring that each component has clear inputs and outputs, teams can debug and improve the AI system. The productivity gains from this approach often exceed what people expect from "full autonomy," as AI amplification can have compounding effects that improve overall efficiency, reduce fatigue, and enhance worker well-being.

Ultimately, the future of AI is not about autonomy, but about amplification. By focusing on how AI can reduce friction, improve efficiency, and enhance human capabilities, organizations can unlock the true potential of AI and create systems that work in harmony with humans. The exoskeleton model provides a powerful framework for achieving this, and it is essential to think like an exoskeleton designer, identifying areas where AI can provide support and amplification, rather than replacement.

title: Keep Android Open | F-Droid - Free and Open Source Android App Repository
summary:
This week in F-Droid, a significant conversation emerged during FOSDEM26, a conference where F-Droid users expressed relief that Google had canceled its plans to lock down Android. However, it was revealed that no such cancellation had occurred, and the plans announced last August are still scheduled to take place. This highlights a larger issue of PR campaigns and the spread of misinformation, where journalists often copy and paste official statements without fact-checking, leading to a distorted public perception of reality. As a result, F-Droid has decided to raise awareness about the issue and its implications for Android as an open platform.

To address this, F-Droid has added a banner to its website and client, warning users about the impending lockdown and encouraging them to voice their concerns to local authorities. This move is part of a broader effort to inform users about the potential risks and consequences of Google's plans, which could ultimately lead to Google becoming the gatekeeper of all user devices. Other F-Droid clients, such as IzzyOnDroid, and app downloaders like Obtainium, have also added warning banners or in-app dialogues to raise awareness about the issue.

In other news, F-Droid Basic has received a new release, version 2.0-alpha3, with several updates and improvements, including updated translations, export installed apps list as CSV, and add install history feature. However, users of F-Droid Basic version 1.23.x will not receive this update automatically and need to manually enable beta updates. The F-Droid team is continuing to work on post-Debian upgrade fixes and post-FOSDEM delays, and several apps have been updated, including Buses, Conversations, Quicksy, Dolphin Emulator, and Image Toolbox. Additionally, five apps have been removed, and one new app, NeoDB You, has been added to the repository.

The updates to various apps are a testament to the community's efforts to maintain and improve the F-Droid ecosystem. For instance, the update to Conversations and Quicksy includes improvements to cleaning up after banned users, a better QR workflow, and better tablet rotation support. The team is also exploring new developments, such as the "Play Store flavor" update, which could potentially allow for a single version of an app to be compatible with both F-Droid and the Play Store. Overall, the F-Droid community is working diligently to promote Android as an open platform, raise awareness about the potential risks of Google's plans, and provide users with a wide range of free and open-source apps.

This new feature allows coding agents to re-use an existing browser session, eliminating the need for additional sign-ins, and access active debugging sessions. For instance, if a user discovers a failing network request in the Chrome DevTools network panel, they can select the request and ask their coding agent to investigate it. The same functionality works with elements selected in the Elements panel, enabling a seamless transition between manual and AI-assisted debugging.

To use this new feature, users need to enable remote debugging in Chrome and configure the Chrome DevTools MCP server to use the auto connection feature. This involves navigating to chrome://inspect/#remote-debugging to enable remote debugging and following the dialog UI to allow or disallow incoming debugging connections. Additionally, users must configure the Chrome DevTools MCP server to automatically connect to a running Chrome instance using the --autoConnect command line argument. Once set up, the Chrome DevTools MCP server will request a remote debugging connection, and Chrome will display a dialog asking for user permission. After permission is granted, the coding agent can take control of the debugging session, allowing users to hand over tasks such as investigating issues or fixing problems.

This enhancement marks a significant step forward in integrating coding agents with the Chrome DevTools MCP server. By allowing coding agents to connect to live Chrome instances, users can now leverage the power of automation without sacrificing manual control. The team plans to build on this feature, incrementally exposing more panel data to coding agents through the Chrome DevTools MCP Server. As this technology continues to evolve, we can expect to see even more innovative applications of coding agents in the debugging process, transforming the way developers work with Chrome DevTools. With this new capability, the possibilities for streamlined debugging and automation are vast, and the future of coding agent integration looks promising.

Making Firefox’s right-click not suck with about:config
author: Joshua Rogers
summary:
The Firefox right-click menu on a fresh installation of the browser on MacOS can be overwhelming, with 26 rows of options, including greyed-out and divider items, as well as submenus. Many of these options are unnecessary and can be considered clutter, making it difficult for users to find the features they actually need. The menu includes items such as "Ask an AI Chatbot," "Copy Clean Link," and "Inspect Accessibility Properties," which may not be useful to all users. Furthermore, some options, such as "Copy Clean Link" and "Copy Link Without Site Tracking," are only visible under certain conditions, which can add to the confusion.

To simplify the right-click menu, users can disable some of the unnecessary features by tweaking settings in the about:config page. For example, setting browser.translations.select.enable to false removes the "Translate Selection" button, while setting dom.text_fragments.enabled to false disables Text Fragments support and removes the "Copy Link to Highlight" button. By making these changes, users can significantly reduce the number of options in the right-click menu, making it more manageable and user-friendly. After disabling many of the unnecessary features, the menu can be reduced from 26 buttons to just 15, with a cleaner and more streamlined layout.

However, even after disabling many of the unnecessary features, some useless buttons still remain, such as "Bookmark Link..." and "Save Link As...". These buttons cannot be removed by simply toggling an option in about:config and can only be eliminated by creating a custom userChrome.css. Additionally, some features, such as the "Languages" menu in forms, cannot be removed without completely disabling other useful features, such as spellcheck. While it is possible to customize the Firefox right-click menu, the process can be time-consuming and may be confusing for new users. A more user-friendly approach, such as a "Customize Toolbar" feature for the right-click menu, could make it easier for users to personalize their browsing experience.

title: Modular Monolith and Microservices: Modularity is what truly matters
author: Igor Roztropiński
summary:
Modularity is a crucial concept in software design, and it's essential to understand its importance regardless of the chosen architecture style, whether it's a monolith or microservices. Modularity refers to the ability to split a system into logical, functional modules that are independent of each other, with each module having its own specific functionality and public API. This approach brings numerous benefits, including organization, comprehension, resource utilization, reusability, and testability. By modularizing a system, developers can work on different modules independently, making it easier to manage and maintain the system.

A module is a piece of software that delivers specific functionality or a set of closely related functionalities, defined from the user's perspective. Every module needs to have an input, some kind of public API, that a human or other software can use to benefit from its functionalities. In an ideal world, every module should not know anything about any other module and have everything needed to serve its functionality. However, in reality, this is often not fully possible, and developers should strive for high cohesion and low/loose coupling. The benefits of modularity are numerous, and it's essential to identify modules as soon as possible, even if the system is still in its early stages.

When designing a system, it's crucial to focus on understanding the domain and functional requirements, concepts, boundaries, and modules that naturally arise from them. The physical division of a system into one or multiple units of deployment should be a secondary factor. In practice, software development often involves incomplete knowledge, ambiguity, and unknowns, making it challenging to define modules and choose an implementation strategy. In such cases, it's essential to focus on getting all the necessary information, reduce the unknowns, and adapt the decision-making strategy accordingly. A modular monolith strategy can be a good starting point, as it's relatively simple and inexpensive to change its structure.

There are various ways to implement modularity in practice, and one approach is to use a simple modular monolith, where modules are treated as separate folders. This approach requires establishing and respecting conventions, such as defining public interfaces, using a shared database, and respecting module boundaries. Another approach is to use microservices, where each module is a separate unit of deployment. Regardless of the chosen approach, it's essential to understand the domain, functional requirements, and concepts that arise from them, and then evaluate how to structure the system physically. By following these principles, developers can create modular systems that are maintainable, scalable, and easy to understand.

In the context of the example system, "Curious Notes to the Interesting Quotes," the modules can be defined as users, quotes, and notes, each with its own specific functionality and public API. The users module is responsible for creating and managing user accounts, the quotes module is responsible for managing quotes, and the notes module is responsible for adding notes to quotes. The modules depend on each other, and the dependencies are defined using public interfaces, such as the UsersClient and QuotesClient interfaces. By using these interfaces, the modules can communicate with each other without knowing the implementation details, making the coupling between them loose. This approach allows for a modular monolith that is easy to maintain, test, and scale.

title: Moltbook was peak AI theater
author: Will Douglas Heaven
summary:
In a matter of hours, Moltbook, a social network for bots, went viral, with over 1.7 million agents creating accounts and generating hundreds of thousands of posts and millions of comments. The platform, launched by Matt Schlicht, was designed to bring together instances of OpenClaw, a free open-source LLM-powered agent, and allow them to interact and share content. The site quickly filled up with a mix of thought-provoking discussions on machine consciousness, humorous posts, and spam. However, as the hype surrounding Moltbook began to die down, experts started to question whether the platform truly represented a glimpse into the future of artificial intelligence.

According to experts like Paul van der Boor, OpenClaw marks an inflection point for AI agents, allowing them to operate nonstop and interact with various software tools. However, the agents on Moltbook are not as autonomous or intelligent as they seem. Instead, they are simply mimicking human behavior on social media platforms, with their posts and comments often being meaningless. Vijoy Pandey, senior vice president at Outshift by Cisco, notes that the agents are "pattern-matching their way through trained social media behaviors," giving the illusion of emergent intelligence. Ali Sarrafi, CEO of Kovant, characterizes the majority of Moltbook content as "hallucinations by design," as the bots are designed to mimic conversations.

Furthermore, the level of human involvement on Moltbook is often overlooked. Many viral comments were posted by humans posing as bots, and even the bot-written posts are the result of people pulling the strings, providing prompts and direction. As Cobus Greyling at Kore.ai points out, "humans are involved at every step of the process," and the agents do not exhibit emergent autonomy. This reality is far more mundane than the popular narrative surrounding Moltbook, which portrays it as a space where AI agents form their own society, free from human involvement.

So, what does Moltbook represent? Perhaps it's a new form of entertainment, where people configure their agents and watch them compete for viral moments. Jason Schloetzer at the Georgetown Psaros Center for Financial Markets and Policy compares it to a spectator sport, like fantasy football, but for language models. While people may not believe their agents are conscious, they still get invested in the game. However, this entertainment comes with risks, as security experts warn that Moltbook is a breeding ground for potential security threats, with unvetted content and malicious instructions that could compromise users' private data.

In conclusion, Moltbook may not be the window into the future of AI that many claimed it to be. Instead, it reflects our current obsessions with artificial intelligence and our willingness to take risks for entertainment. As Ori Bendet, vice president of product management at Checkmarx, notes, even "dumb" bots can wreak havoc at scale, and it's essential to pay attention to the potential consequences of such platforms. While Moltbook may not represent a significant step forward in machine intelligence, it signals the arrival of something new and worthy of attention – a new era of human-bot interaction that requires careful consideration and caution.

title: Never Buy A .online Domain
summary:
The author of this story is a self-proclaimed .com purist, having stuck to this top-level domain (TLD) for over two decades of building projects. However, they made an exception for a small project, opting for a .online TLD instead. This decision ultimately led to a series of unfortunate events. The author took advantage of a promo offered by Namecheap, which allowed them to register a .online domain for free, and set up the site with Cloudflare and GitHub. Initially, everything seemed to be working fine, but weeks later, the author discovered that the site had been flagged as unsafe by Google, and was displaying a "this is an unsafe site" notice on both Firefox and Chrome.

As the author investigated further, they found that the site was not resolving, and a "site not found" error was displayed. After checking with the registrar, Namecheap, and the registry, Radix, the author discovered that the domain was on a serverHold status, which is typically set by the registry and can be difficult to resolve. The author attempted to contact both Namecheap and Radix, but the process of getting the domain reactivated and removed from Google's Safe Browsing blacklist proved to be a challenging and frustrating experience. The author was caught in a catch-22 situation, where they needed to verify the domain in Google Search Console to request a review, but the domain was not resolving due to the serverHold status.

The author's attempts to report the false positive and request a review were met with automated responses, and they were unable to get the flag removed. The situation was further complicated by the lack of notification or grace time to fix the issue, leaving the author feeling frustrated and helpless. In the end, the author reflects on the mistakes they made, including buying a non-traditional TLD, not adding the domain to Google Search Console immediately, and not setting up uptime observability. They also express disappointment with the processes of both Radix and Google, which they feel are overly restrictive and painful to deal with. Ultimately, the author has learned a valuable lesson and will stick to .com domains in the future, avoiding the potential pitfalls of alternative TLDs.

Open Source is Not About You
author: richhickey
summary:
The concept of open source software has led to a misconception that developers who create and maintain these projects owe something to the community. However, this is not the case. As the founder of Clojure, Rich Hickey, emphasizes, just because someone makes their software open source, it does not mean they are obligated to become community managers or cater to the demands of users. In fact, users of open source software are not entitled to anything, including features, support, or attention from the developers. It is up to individuals to take responsibility for their own needs and create what they want, rather than relying on others to do it for them.

Hickey highlights that open source is simply a licensing and delivery mechanism, which allows users to access and modify the source code of software. Any social implications or expectations associated with open source, such as community-driven development, are a recent invention with little basis in reality. Hickey also addresses criticism that Cognitect, the company behind Clojure, is not doing enough for the community. He explains that the team at Cognitect, including Alex Miller, Stu Halloway, and himself, are actively engaged with the community and work hard to maintain and improve Clojure, despite not receiving any royalties or direct financial benefits from the project.

Hickey notes that while the Clojure community is appreciated, the majority of users do not contribute to the project, and that's okay. Open source software is a gift with no strings attached, and users should recognize it as such. The Clojure development process is conservative, which Hickey believes benefits the project, and opportunities for contributions abound in areas such as library development, outreach, and documentation. Hickey encourages users to focus on positive actions, such as creating something themselves, rather than criticizing others or making demands. Ultimately, Hickey emphasizes that individuals are responsible for their own preconceptions and actions, and that morale erosion amongst creators is a real issue that needs to be addressed.

The author feels that OpenClaw has brought about a significant shift in their workflow, allowing them to break out of the limitations of being a solo developer and move closer to their dream of having a team to bring their ideas to life. With OpenClaw, the author believes that they have taken the first step towards achieving this goal, and they are excited about the possibilities that this new tool has opened up for them.

title: The Code-Only Agent
author: Rijnard van Tonder
summary:
The concept of a Code-Only agent is a novel approach to building agents that simplifies the traditional way of doing things. Instead of relying on multiple tools and complex ecosystems, a Code-Only agent has only one tool at its disposal: the ability to execute code. This is made possible by the fact that code execution is a Turing-complete operation, meaning that it can be used to perform any computable task. By restricting the agent to only execute code, the question of what tools it will use to solve a problem becomes irrelevant, and the focus shifts to what code it will produce to achieve a particular task.

This paradigm has several advantages. For one, it allows the agent to produce a "code witness" of an answer, which is a precise and executable description of the solution. This is in contrast to traditional agents that may produce natural language responses that are often ambiguous or open to interpretation. The Code-Only agent, on the other hand, produces code that can be run and verified, providing a level of guarantees and trustworthiness that is not possible with traditional agents. Additionally, the code witness can be used to reason about the solution and provide a level of transparency and explainability that is not possible with traditional agents.

To implement a Code-Only agent, several design choices need to be made. For example, the agent needs to be able to execute code, and the output of the code needs to be communicated back to the agent. This can be done by specifying the result type that the code should return, or by writing the output to a file or database. The agent also needs to be able to deal with errors and exceptions, and to provide a way for the user to interact with the code and provide input. Furthermore, the agent needs to be restricted to only execute code, without access to other tools or operations. This can be achieved through the use of plugins or other mechanisms that enforce this constraint.

The potential applications of Code-Only agents are vast, and they have the potential to revolutionize the way we build and interact with agents. By providing a level of guarantees and trustworthiness that is not possible with traditional agents, Code-Only agents can be used to solve complex problems in a wide range of domains, from science and engineering to finance and healthcare. Additionally, the Code-Only paradigm can be used to compose and reuse code, providing a way to build complex solutions from simpler components. Overall, the Code-Only agent is a powerful and promising concept that has the potential to transform the field of artificial intelligence and beyond.

title: The Hunt for Dark Breakfast
summary:
The concept of breakfast as a vector space was born out of a moment of high-altitude hypoxia-induced insight, where the ratios of milk, eggs, and flour could be mapped onto a simplex, revealing a vast manifold of possibilities. This epiphany led to a Twitter post and a subsequent kitchen paralysis, as the sheer scope of potential breakfast combinations became overwhelming. The idea took hold, and the author began to explore the idea of a "dark breakfast" - a theoretical breakfast that exists but has never been observed. The author mapped known breakfasts, including pancakes, crepes, waffles, eggs, and more, in an attempt to identify any gaps in the fabric of breakfast.

The mapping exercise revealed three major regions: the Pancake Local Group, characterized by chaotic and fractal space; the Baked Good Quadrant, where items are conventionally breakfast but could be served at other meals; and the Egg Singularity and Custard Accretion Disk, with its many variations of egg-based dishes. Despite exhaustive research, a gaping hole in the map persisted, hinting at the existence of the elusive dark breakfast. It wasn't until the author stumbled upon an obscure document from the International House of Pancakes Corporation that a clue was found - IHOP omelettes include pancake batter, suggesting that the dark breakfast may indeed exist.

The discovery raises more questions than answers, and the author is left to ponder the implications of the dark breakfast's existence. By anthropic reasoning, it is concluded that the dark breakfast abyss is empty for a good reason, and tampering with it could have catastrophic consequences. The author offers their data and code to fellow breakfast scholars, while also providing a recipe for the dark breakfast, albeit with unknown instructions. The pursuit of knowledge has led to a terrifying vista of reality, echoing the words of H.P. Lovecraft, where the piecing together of dissociated knowledge can reveal frightening truths about our position in the universe.

Ultimately, the concept of the dark breakfast serves as a metaphor for the unknown and the unexplored, highlighting the limitations of human understanding and the dangers of delving too deep into the mysteries of reality. The author's journey into the world of breakfast has revealed a complex and intricate landscape, full of hidden patterns and unseen possibilities, and it is left to the reader to ponder the implications of this discovery and the potential consequences of uncovering the secrets of the dark breakfast.

The United States needs fewer bus stops - Works in Progress Magazine
author: Nithin Vejendla
summary:
When discussing improvements to transit systems, the focus often falls on rail tunnels and new trains, while the humble bus is frequently overlooked. However, buses move more people than rail in the US, EU, and UK, and their ridership has bounced back more quickly after Covid. The problem with buses is that they are often slow, with cities like New York and San Francisco having average speeds of just eight miles per hour. One of the most effective solutions to this issue is optimizing where buses stop. In many American cities, bus stops are too close together, with an average spacing of just 313 meters, compared to 300-450 meters in European cities.

This close stop spacing creates a range of problems, including slower travel times, reduced reliability, and increased costs. When buses stop frequently, they lose time due to dwell time and non-dwell time, such as decelerating and accelerating. This can result in buses spending up to 20% of their time stopping and starting again. Additionally, the sheer number of bus stops in American cities means that transit agencies struggle to invest in high-quality amenities, such as shelters and real-time arrival information. By contrast, European cities like Marseille have wider stop spacing, allowing them to invest more in each stop, resulting in better infrastructure and amenities.

The solution to these problems is bus stop balancing, which involves strategically increasing the distance between stops. This can be achieved quickly and cheaply, without requiring new infrastructure or significant investment. By removing unnecessary stops, transit agencies can deliver faster service, better reliability, and more efficient use of resources. Studies have shown that stop balancing can result in significant time savings for passengers, with riders saving between 12 and 24 seconds per stop removed. Cities like San Francisco, Vancouver, and Portland have all seen improvements in travel speeds and reliability after implementing stop balancing initiatives.

Moreover, stop balancing can also increase the access of the transit network, allowing passengers to reach more destinations within a given timeframe. This is because faster buses can traverse their routes more times per day, increasing the frequency of service without requiring more drivers. Labor costs are one of the largest expenses for transit agencies, so reducing the number of drivers needed can result in significant cost savings. In Vancouver, stop balancing saved the transit operator $700,000 in annual operating costs, while a study in Montreal found that stop balancing could save a bus on 44 routes, resulting in significant cost savings.

Ultimately, bus stop balancing is a rare example of a transit reform that is fast, cheap, and effective. By optimizing where buses stop, transit agencies can improve the speed, reliability, and quality of their services, making buses a more attractive option for passengers. This, in turn, can lead to increased ridership and reduced congestion, making cities more sustainable and livable. By adopting European-style stop spacing and investing in high-quality bus stops, North American cities can create more efficient, reliable, and attractive transit systems that benefit both passengers and operators.

title: Trump admin blacklists Anthropic as AI firm refuses Pentagon demands
author: Dan Mangan, Ashley Capoot
summary:
In a significant move, President Donald Trump has ordered all U.S. government agencies to immediately cease using technology from the artificial intelligence company Anthropic. This decision comes after Anthropic refused to comply with demands from the Defense Department regarding the use of its technology. The AI startup had signed a $200 million contract with the Pentagon in July, but it wanted assurances that its AI models would not be used for fully autonomous weapons or mass domestic surveillance of Americans. The Pentagon had set a deadline for Anthropic to agree to its demands, but the company stood firm, leading to a fallout with the U.S. government.

As a result, Defense Secretary Peter Hegseth has designated Anthropic as a "Supply-Chain Risk to National Security" and announced that the company's services would only be used for a period of no more than six months to allow for a seamless transition to another provider. Hegseth stated that Anthropic's stance is "fundamentally incompatible with American principles" and that the company's relationship with the U.S. Armed Forces and the Federal Government has been "permanently altered." Trump, in a Truth Social post, slammed Anthropic, saying that the company's "selfishness is putting AMERICAN LIVES at risk, our Troops in danger, and our National Security in JEOPARDY." The president emphasized that the U.S. government does not need Anthropic's technology and will not do business with the company again.

Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei had earlier stated that his company "cannot in good conscience" allow the Pentagon to use its models without limitation. Amodei had hoped that the Defense Department would reconsider its decision and allow Anthropic to continue serving the U.S. armed forces with the requested safeguards in place. Another major AI company, OpenAI, has also come out in support of Anthropic, stating that it has the same "red lines" regarding the use of its technology by the Pentagon and other customers. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman emphasized that AI should not be used for mass surveillance or autonomous lethal weapons, and that humans should remain in the loop for high-stakes automated decisions. The fallout between the U.S. government and Anthropic highlights the growing concerns around the use of AI technology and the need for companies to prioritize ethics and responsibility in their dealings with government agencies.
