title: What happens when coding agents stop feeling like dialup?
What I learned building an opinionated and minimal coding agent
summary:
The author of the text has been using Large Language Models (LLMs) for assisted coding over the past three years, evolving from copying and pasting code into ChatGPT to using more advanced tools like Claude Code. However, they found that Claude Code became too complex and feature-rich, breaking their workflows and changing model behavior with each release. The author, preferring simple and predictable tools, decided to build their own coding agent harness. They created a set of components, including pi-ai, a unified LLM API with multi-provider support, pi-agent-core, an agent loop that handles tool execution and event streaming, and pi-tui, a minimal terminal UI framework.

The author's philosophy was to only build what they needed, resulting in a minimalist approach. They focused on creating a unified LLM API that abstracts away the differences between various providers, such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google. The pi-ai API supports features like streaming, tool calling, and context handoffs between providers. The author also implemented a context serialization and deserialization system, allowing for seamless switching between models and providers. For example, they can start with one model, switch to another, and have the new model understand the context and thinking traces from the previous model. The author achieved this by converting thinking traces into content blocks and using signed blobs to maintain context.

The author also created a model registry that parses data from OpenRouter and models.dev, generating TypeScript types for specifying models. This allows for a typesafe way of working with different models and providers. Additionally, the author implemented a system for aborting requests and returning partial results, which is essential for integrating LLMs into production systems. They also introduced a structured split tool results system, which allows tools to return separate content blocks for the LLM and UI display, making it easier to parse and display tool outputs. Overall, the author's goal was to create a simple, flexible, and customizable coding agent harness that meets their specific needs and workflow.

title: When a Bug Saved the Company
When Did AI Take Over Hacker News? | Blog | Zach Perkel
When internal hostnames are leaked to the clown
summary:
The situation described begins with the setup of a network-attached storage (NAS) device on a home network. The NAS has a preference for running in https mode, so a wildcard certificate is installed, specifically for a sub-zone of a domain that isn't used for any significant public internet activities. This sub-zone, such as *.nothing-special.whatever.example.com, is quite deep and not directly exposed to the public. To access the NAS, an entry is made in the hosts file, mapping the NAS's IP address to its designated hostname, allowing it to be accessed through a browser.

However, things take an interesting turn when the user starts noticing requests coming from the outside world to their server, using the same hostname that was only defined in the laptop's hosts file. This discovery is made possible because a wildcard DNS entry had been set up to point to a controlled machine, essentially creating a trap to catch any unexpected traffic. It becomes apparent that every time the NAS is loaded, a Google Cloud Platform (GCP) host attempts to connect, presenting the same hostname that was thought to be private. This raises concerns about the potential sensitivity of the hostname used, as it could reveal internal information.

Further investigation reveals that the NAS's web interface has a feature that phones home, sending stack traces to sentry.io. It's found that the browser is communicating with sentry.io, sharing the hostname of the internal storage box. Moreover, sentry.io then attempts to establish a TLS connection back to the NAS, although no requests are made. To mitigate this situation, the user employs Little Snitch to block the entire domain for any application on the machine, effectively shutting down this unintended communication channel. The story ends with a hint that this sentry reporting mechanism could potentially be exploited to scan other hosts, leaving the exploration of this vulnerability as an exercise for the reader.

title: Why No AI Games?

summary:
The advent of AI technology has been expected to revolutionize the gaming industry, giving rise to new and innovative game experiences. However, despite the immense potential of AI, the past five years have seen little to no significant new AI-based game experiences. The author of the text reflects on this puzzle, citing a few examples of AI-centric games such as AI Dungeon, Death by AI, and Suck Up!, but notes that these games have not lived up to the hype. AI Dungeon, for instance, was seen as a novelty due to its text-generation capabilities, but its gameplay was limited and did not offer a truly immersive experience. Death by AI, on the other hand, was a party game that used AI to present scenarios and determine player outcomes, but its success was short-lived.

The author also mentions other semi-viral demos of AI-powered game engines, such as Drunk Minecraft and Google's Genie 3, but notes that these engines have not produced anything particularly mind-blowing or novel. This lack of innovation in AI-based games is puzzling, given the potential of AI to create new and exciting game experiences. The author suggests that there are several reasons for this, including business model issues, cultural taboos, and the inherent nature of fun. For instance, the cost of using third-party AI services can be prohibitively expensive, making it difficult for developers to create sustainable business models for their games. Furthermore, the video game audience has largely rejected the idea of generative AI in games, making it challenging for developers to create games that appeal to a wide audience.

The author ultimately concludes that the problem lies in the nature of fun itself. While AI models are powerful and fascinating, they do not seem to be an intrinsic source of fun. The author suggests that this is because AI models are too similar to human behavior, which is not inherently fun. Instead, games derive their fun from simple behaviors and deterministic rules, which allow for emergence and complexity to arise. The author notes that this realization has led to a newfound appreciation for the traditional video game logic, which relies on brittle, deterministic rules to create engaging experiences. However, the author remains hopeful that the future will bring new and innovative AI-based game experiences, and intends to continue exploring the possibilities of AI in gaming. The author's conclusion is not a dismissal of AI's potential in gaming, but rather a call to re-examine the assumptions about what makes games fun and how AI can be used to create new and exciting experiences.

title: Why the KeePass format should be based on SQLite
author: Mohammed Ketab
summary:
KeePass, a renowned password manager, has been hindered by its KDBX file format, which has been in use since 2007. The format, based on XML, has become a persistent source of friction for developers and users, particularly when it comes to adopting modern security and convenience features. One of the primary issues with the KDBX format is its brittle schema, which makes it difficult to add new features without causing breaking changes for older clients or third-party clients. This has led to the use of custom attributes to implement new features, resulting in a cluttered and incompatible landscape. For instance, the implementation of Time-Based One-Time Passwords (TOTP) and passkey support has been problematic, with different clients using different field names and storage methods.

The KDBX format also suffers from storage inefficiencies, particularly with regards to custom icons, which are stored as base64 encoded strings within the XML file. This causes a significant increase in file size and memory usage, leading to slow access and save speeds, especially on mobile devices. Furthermore, the entire file is loaded into memory, which can cause problems if the program crashes or power is halted mid-save. These issues have led to the development of alternate saving methods, such as backup files, to mitigate the risks of data corruption or loss.

The governance structure of the KDBX format has also been a point of contention, with Dominik Reichl, the founder of KeePass, maintaining the format and its iterations for an extended period. While his focus on conservatism and compatibility is appreciated, it has limited the ability to expand the features of the format to accommodate modern use cases and development practices. The lack of a democratic and open decision-making process has led to a situation where the vast majority of users within the KeePass ecosystem are subject to the decisions of the original program, despite the existence of more popular and actively developed forks.

A potential solution to these problems is to adopt a new storage format, such as SQLite with SQLCipher, which would allow for a more flexible and efficient storage of data. This would enable the implementation of new features, such as granular global history and versioning, complex group relations, and specific entry types for payment cards, without causing breaking changes. The use of SQLite would also reduce database sizes, memory usage, and improve access and save speeds, making it an attractive option for the KeePass ecosystem. Additionally, it would provide an opportunity to establish a more democratic and open governance structure, aligning the project management with the needs of the community.

title: Worlds: A Simulation Engine for Agentic Pentesting
author: Shane Caldwell and Max Harley
summary:
The security domain is hindered by a significant training data problem, which prevents the creation of small, task-specific models that can automate security operations. The primary issue is the lack of access to high-quality environments and data, as much of it is siloed in private companies or unusable due to compliance. Current solutions, such as using handcrafted labs, building infrastructure from scratch, or leveraging large networks, are insufficient and often impractical. For instance, handcrafted lab environments like GOAD are excellent for humans and testing agent mechanics, but each configuration is static, slow to set up, and difficult to reset. Building Windows networks from scratch requires specialized knowledge, significant memory, and substantial cost, making it a costly endeavor.

To address this challenge, the concept of using programmatically simulated computer networks to train security agents has been explored. However, this approach has been hindered by the "Sim2Real gap," where the simulation environment does not accurately reflect real-world scenarios. To bridge this gap, a simulation engine called Worlds has been developed, which generates realistic penetration testing trajectories across arbitrary Active Directory networks. Worlds addresses the Sim2Real gap by ensuring that actions and observations in the simulation match reality, and by modeling relevant and accurate state dynamics of the environment. For example, Worlds simulates the graph nature of Active Directory, including credential attacks, delegation abuse, ACL abuse, and AD CS abuse, which are common misconfigurations that can be exploited.

Worlds consists of two conceptual layers: manifests and simulation. Manifests define the bounds of hosts, services, principals, and their relationships, while the simulation layer produces complete trajectories of agent tool calls. The simulation engine can generate arbitrary amounts of diverse tool call sequences for arbitrary networks, without ever leaving the CPU. Additionally, Worlds can augment the dataset with synthetic data generation techniques, such as generating reasoning traces that mirror what a real agent would produce. These reasoning traces are generated by a language model given the full trajectory context, and are essential for creating a complete and realistic training dataset.

The outcome of using Worlds is a fine-tuned 8B model that can compromise a GOAD domain using purely synthetic training data. This achievement demonstrates the potential of using simulated environments to train security models, and highlights the importance of addressing the Sim2Real gap. The use of Worlds has significant implications for the security domain, as it enables the creation of small, task-specific models that can automate security operations without requiring large amounts of real-world data. With Worlds, it is possible to generate high-quality training data for any network configuration, without the need for distilling larger model trajectories, standing up actual hosts, aggregating customer data, or creating digital twins. This innovation has the potential to revolutionize the field of security, enabling more efficient and effective automated penetration testing and network operations.
